user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction which failed due to Defendant's default. Plead total failure of consideration under Section 65 of Indian Contract Act. Claim refund with interest and costs. Include cause of action paragraph with accrual date, continuing nature of cause of action, valuation, court fee, and proper verification clause. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction that failed due to the Defendant's default. The suit claims total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, seeking refund with interest and costs.

slots.value: Recovery of Money / Refund of Advance

missing_fields: Date of cause of action accrual
assumptions: Jurisdiction lies with the District Court based on valuation or cause of action
Suit is within the limitation period

Where the contract between the parties has merged in the order of the court, the courts freedom to act to 
further the ends of justice would not stand curtailed. Although the court would not rewrite a contract between 
the parties, but it would relieve against a forfeiture clause. By virtue of the courts order in terms of the 
compromise, the time for deposit stipulated by the parties became the time allowed by the court and this gave 
the court the jurisdiction to extend time. But the time would be granted in rare cases to prevent manifest 
injustice and it would not be extended ordinarily, nor for the mere asking.16
In a recovery certificate proceedings under the Recovery of Debts Due to Banks and Financial Institutions Act, 
1993, the Bank agree for one-time settlement and the parties settled their claim before Lok Adalat. The Debt 
Recovery Tribunal passed detailed order recording the Award of the Lok Adalat. The borrower, however, failed 
to repay the amount as per Award passed by the Lok Adalat inspite of sufficient time as also extended time. It 
was only after the Bank proceeded recovery certificate the borrower started filing applications, after much 
delay. It was held that the

suit was barred by limitation in terms of Art. 58 of the Limitation Act, 1963. The High Court, 
therefore, in our opinion, ex facie committed an error in arriving on the aforementioned finding.56

has no jurisdiction to entertain the suit in view of the bar created under Recovery of Debts Due to Banks and

the suit was barred by statute, it was held liable to be rejected under O 7, r 11(d) of the Code.57

for encashment, it was held by the Delhi High Court that the reliefs sought will amount to restraining the defendant 
from instituting civil or criminal proceedings against the plaintiff on account of non-encashment of cheques which 
would amount to hampering access to justice. The reliefs sought is barred by law being directly hit by the provisions 
of s 41 (b), (d) of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 and as such the injunction can be refused. However, in the light of

Where the respondent is in possession of the suit property, a suit for injunction simpliciter without claiming

application was held to be hopelessly barred by limitation and was held not maintainable.43

the valuation for jurisdiction in certain suits is the same. Under s 7 cl (iv)(f) of the Court Fees Act, 1870, the

jurisdictional purposes should be on the basis of annual rent or profit. Suit so valued along with the claim of 
damage exceeding pecuniary jurisdiction of the court is liable to be returned for prosecution before the proper 
court.5

In case where the subject matter is not capable of being estimated at a money value, it is provided by s 9 of the 
Suits Valuation Act, 1887, that the value of the suit for purposes of jurisdiction is what the High Court may 
specify by rules made under that section. Where no rules are made, the High Courts of Allahabad and Calcutta 
have said that the safest and most convenient course is to treat the valuation made by the plaintiff as prima

the above rule was laid down were suits for restitution of conjugal rights. In a subsequent case, however, the

appropriate in suits for restitution of conjugal rights, it was not so in suits which affected property, and that the 
best rule in such cases was to value the suits according to the value of the property liable to be affected 
thereby.54 Thus, a suit to compel registration of an instrument whether the instrument be registrable

the right of maintaining a revision before High Court under s 115, Code of Civil Procedure. No doubt, as 
provided under sub-s (4)(a) of s 11 of the Karnataka Court-fees and Suits Valuation Act, 1958, he will have a 
right to raise such an issue before the court of appeal if an occasion arises, and in such a situation the 
procedure laid down under the said sub-section will have to be followed for adjudicating and for recovery of the 
deficit court fee and refund of the excess court fee but the revision against the said order is not maintainable.91

sections.body:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

examples.bad: The cause of action arose within the jurisdiction of this Court.
examples.good: The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which falls within the territorial jurisdiction of this Court. Further, the advance amount was transferred from the Plaintiff's account at {{BANK_NAME}}, {{BANK_BRANCH}} situated within the local limits of this Court's jurisdiction.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) How payment was made — specific amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

examples.good: Despite repeated demands, the Defendant neither performed his obligations under the agreement nor returned the advance amount of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- to the Plaintiff. The Defendant's last communication was on {{DATE}}, wherein he sought further time but made no payment whatsoever.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. For each statutory provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea (e.g., Section 73 ICA as alternative to Section 65 ICA) using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading: pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments (e.g., no part performance was rendered, no forfeiture clause exists, no adjustment made, no counter-claim exists). Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list.

examples.bad: Under Section 65, the Defendant is bound to restore the amount.
examples.good: Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 provides that when an agreement is discovered to be void, or when a contract becomes void, any person who has received any advantage under such agreement or contract is bound to restore it to the person from whom he received it. In the present case, the business transaction between the parties has resulted in a total failure of consideration inasmuch as the Defendant received Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- but provided no benefit whatsoever to the Plaintiff.

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited
Legal consequence explained (not just section named)

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

examples.bad: Cause of action arose on X date.
examples.good: The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to perform his obligations under the business transaction despite being bound to do so, and the Plaintiff's right to claim refund of the advance amount thereby accrued. The cause of action further and continuously arose on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice demanding refund, failed, neglected, and refused to repay the said amount.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

filled_sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant first established a commercial relationship in connection with a proposed business venture, and on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} the parties reduced their understanding to writing in the form of an agreement, the text of which is set out in Annexure A. The agreement records that the parties contemplated carrying out the venture and defined the framework within which the Plaintiff would provide consideration for the venture while the Defendant undertook to perform certain obligations in relation to the venture.

2. The terms of the agreement included that the Plaintiff would advance Rs. 20,00,000 as consideration for the venture, the amount and purpose of which are evidenced by Annexure A, and that the advance would be governed by the performance timeline set out in the agreement. Annexures to the agreement describe the scope of the venture and the conditions under which the advance would be utilized or refunded depending on the Defendant’s performance.

3. The Defendant undertook to perform the venture by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} in accordance with the terms of the agreement, and the Plaintiff remained ready to cooperate and advance further steps as contemplated by the parties’ arrangement. The timeline for performance is recorded in Annexure A, which forms the basis for the Defendant’s obligations in relation to the venture.

4. On {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff remitted Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant as consideration for the venture, and the Plaintiff’s payment is evidenced by Annexure B (payment proof such as bank statement/receipt/UTR). The transaction was completed in the manner described in Annexure B and in line with the parties’ understanding as set out in Annexure A.

5. By {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform the agreed obligations or to refund the advance as contemplated by the agreement. Despite the Plaintiff’s readiness to proceed and the Defendant’s stated obligations under the contract, there was no delivery of the agreed goods or services, nor was there any refund of the Rs. 20,00,000 advance by the Defendant by that date. The Defendant’s default is evidenced by the events described above and is consistent with the failure to perform as required under Annexure A.

6. On {{NOTICE_DATE}}, the Plaintiff served a legal notice demanding repayment of Rs. 20,00,000 with interest and costs in relation to the default, in accordance with the notice procedures described in Annexure C. The Defendant replied to the notice on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}, and the response is contained in Annexure D. The notice and reply document the parties’ communication and the Defendant’s position in response to the claim for refund and interest.

7. As of {{FILING_DATE}}, the Plaintiff remains unpaid of Rs. 20,00,000 and the cause of action arising from the breach continues to subsist. The cause of action accrues on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and remains actionable, with the Plaintiff invoking the remedy of recovery of the advance amount along with interest and costs. The Plaintiff’s case is supported by the documents referenced above, including Annexure A (Agreement), Annexure B (Payment proof), Annexure C (Legal notice), and Annexure D (Reply to legal notice). The plaint is valued for the purposes of court fee at Rs. 20,00,000, and appropriate court fee and costs will be sought in the suit. Verification to follow as per the Rules.
 
Annexures referenced: Annexure A, Annexure B, Annexure C, Annexure D.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text:
Section 65 Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates the obligation of restitution where an agreement or contract yields a benefit to one party but the consideration fails or the contract becomes void; the legal rule is that any person who has received any advantage under such an agreement or contract is bound to restore it to the person from whom he received it. The triggering conditions are (i) the existence of a contract for a business transaction; (ii) the plaintiff’s payment of consideration to the other party, here Rs. 20,00,000; (iii) the contract failing or becoming void due to the defendant’s default resulting in total failure of the consideration; and (iv) a demand for restitution by the aggrieved party. Under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, there is a total failure of consideration where the promisor receives money but the promised performance never occurs, entitling the payee to restitution; applying this rule to the present facts, the Plaintiff’s right to sue for the refund of Rs. 20,00,000 crystallized on the date of the Defendant’s default and the Plaintiff is entitled to recover the amount together with interest and costs. The cause of action further accrued on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Plaintiff served a legal notice demanding refund of the advance, but the Defendant did not comply with the demand and continued to withhold the sum, thereby sustaining the breach and extending the duration of the cause of action. The claim remains a continuing one because the Defendant continues to retain Rs. 20,00,000 without lawful justification, and the Plaintiff remains ready and willing to recover the amount with interest at the rate permitted by law and costs, until the refund is made. The cause of action accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and is continuing.

Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 at 12% per annum for the period from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} on the ground that the amount was advanced for a commercial transaction and the Defendant’s wrongful retention constitutes breach of the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Order 37 CPC, Civil Procedure Code, 1908)


BETWEEN

The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, occupation {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}} (hereinafter referred to as the Plaintiff), and the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, occupation {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}} (hereinafter referred to as the Defendant), are the parties to this suit filed before the District Court at {{DISTRICT}}. The Plaintiff is sui juris and competent to sue and be sued.

On or about {{ACCRUAL_DATE}}, the Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant at the Defendant's address as an advance for a business transaction evidenced by Annexure A. The Defendant undertook to perform the transaction but failed to do so, resulting in total failure of consideration. The cause of action is of a continuing nature since the amount remains unpaid and the Plaintiff continues to suffer.

The suit is valued at Rs. 20,00,000 for the purposes of court fee and jurisdiction, and the Court fee payable on such value is calculated at the applicable rates. Annexure B contains the payment proof.

Verification: I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff, do hereby verify that the contents of this plaint are true and correct to the best of my knowledge and belief. Verified at {{CITY}} on {{VERIFICATION_DATE}}.


JURISDICTION

Territorial jurisdiction is established as the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_PLACE}}, which lies within the local limits of the District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}, as of the date of filing. The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, paid Rs. 20,00,000 by way of advance on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} at {{PAYMENT_PLACE}} in pursuance of a business agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} executed at {{EXECUTION_PLACE}} between {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}} and {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}. The cause of action arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} at {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_PLACE}} in relation to the agreement when the Defendant defaulted by not refunding the advance or performing the contracted obligations, and the cause of action is continuing as of {{FILING_DATE}}. The present suit seeks refund of Rs. 20,00,000 with interest and costs. The pecuniary value of the suit is Rs. 20,00,000, which lies within the monetary limits of the District Court for the State of {{STATE}}.


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant first established a commercial relationship in connection with a proposed business venture, and on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} the parties reduced their understanding to writing in the form of an agreement, the text of which is set out in Annexure A. The agreement records that the parties contemplated carrying out the venture and defined the framework within which the Plaintiff would provide consideration for the venture while the Defendant undertook to perform certain obligations in relation to the venture.

2. The terms of the agreement included that the Plaintiff would advance Rs. 20,00,000 as consideration for the venture, the amount and purpose of which are evidenced by Annexure A, and that the advance would be governed by the performance timeline set out in the agreement. Annexures to the agreement describe the scope of the venture and the conditions under which the advance would be utilized or refunded depending on the Defendants performance.

3. The Defendant undertook to perform the venture by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} in accordance with the terms of the agreement, and the Plaintiff remained ready to cooperate and advance further steps as contemplated by the parties arrangement. The timeline for performance is recorded in Annexure A, which forms the basis for the Defendant	s obligations in relation to the venture.

4. On {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff remitted Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant as consideration for the venture, and the Plaintiffs payment is evidenced by Annexure B (payment proof such as bank statement/receipt/UTR). The transaction was completed in the manner described in Annexure B and in line with the parties understanding as set out in Annexure A.

5. By {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform the agreed obligations or to refund the advance as contemplated by the agreement. Despite the Plaintiffs readiness to proceed and the Defendant	s stated obligations under the contract, there was no delivery of the agreed goods or services, nor was there any refund of the Rs. 20,00,000 advance by the Defendant by that date. The Defendants default is evidenced by the events described above and is consistent with the failure to perform as required under Annexure A.

6. On {{NOTICE_DATE}}, the Plaintiff served a legal notice demanding repayment of Rs. 20,00,000 with interest and costs in relation to the default, in accordance with the notice procedures described in Annexure C. The Defendant replied to the notice on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}, and the response is contained in Annexure D. The notice and reply document the partiesommunication and the Defendants position in response to the claim for refund and interest.

7. As of {{FILING_DATE}}, the Plaintiff remains unpaid of Rs. 20,00,000 and the cause of action arising from the breach continues to subsist. The cause of action accrues on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and remains actionable, with the Plaintiff invoking the remedy of recovery of the advance amount along with interest and costs. The Plaintiffs case is supported by the documents referenced above, including Annexure A (Agreement), Annexure B (Payment proof), Annexure C (Legal notice), and Annexure D (Reply to legal notice). The plaint is valued for the purposes of court fee at Rs. 20,00,000, and appropriate court fee and costs will be sought in the suit. Verification to follow as per the Rules.
 
Annexures referenced: Annexure A, Annexure B, Annexure C, Annexure D.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

Section 65 Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates the obligation of restitution where an agreement or contract yields a benefit to one party but the consideration fails or the contract becomes void; the legal rule is that any person who has received any advantage under such an agreement or contract is bound to restore it to the person from whom he received it. The triggering conditions are (i) the existence of a contract for a business transaction; (ii) the plaintiffs payment of consideration to the other party, here Rs. 20,00,000; (iii) the contract failing or becoming void due to the defendants default resulting in total failure of the consideration; and (iv) a demand for restitution by the aggrieved party. In the present case, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, on {{ADVANCE_DATE}} at {{ADVANCE_PLACE}} paid Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, as an advance for a business transaction evidenced by Annexure A; the transaction failed due to the Defendant�s default, thereby constituting total failure of consideration; accordingly, the Defendant is bound to refund the amount to the Plaintiff. The accrual date of the cause of action is {{ACCRUAL_DATE}}, and the cause of action is of continuing nature until the amount is refunded, with interest and costs as permissible by law. For court fee purposes the suit is valued at Rs. 20,00,000, and the Plaintiff seeks interest on the amount from accrual date and costs. A proper verification clause will accompany the plaint in accordance with law; the pleading is supported by Annexure A (the underlying agreement) and Annexure B (payment proof). The Plaintiff pre-emptively neutralizes likely defenses: there was no part performance by the Plaintiff, there is no forfeiture clause in the contract, no adjustment has been made, and there is no counter-claim by the Defendant. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if the Court does not accept a total failure of consideration as the sole basis for relief, the Plaintiff pleads the same remedy under Section 65 as an alternative, with interest and costs, to the extent permissible.

The relief sought is restitution of Rs. 20,00,000 along with interest and costs, grounded squarely on Section 65 Indian Contract Act, and the case is framed to meet the accrual and continuing nature of the cause of action while ensuring proper valuation and verifiable pleading.


CAUSE OF ACTION

The cause of action in this suit first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, which corresponds to the date on which the Defendant failed to perform its obligations under the contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} after the Plaintiff had paid Rs. 20,00,000 as an advance on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to the Defendant at place_names, thereby causing total failure of consideration for which the Plaintiff seeks restitution. The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, paid Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant as an advance for a business transaction contemplated under the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, and the Defendant undertook to perform certain duties but defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, thereby depriving the Plaintiff of any benefit promised under the contract and resulting in a complete failure of consideration. Under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, there is a total failure of consideration where the promisor receives money but the promised performance never occurs, entitling the payee to restitution; applying this rule to the present facts, the Plaintiffs right to sue for the refund of Rs. 20,00,000 crystallized on the date of the Defendant�s default and the Plaintiff is entitled to recover the amount together with interest and costs. The cause of action further accrued on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Plaintiff served a legal notice demanding refund of the advance, but the Defendant did not comply with the demand and continued to withhold the sum, thereby sustaining the breach and extending the duration of the cause of action. The claim remains a continuing one because the Defendant continues to retain Rs. 20,00,000 without lawful justification, and the Plaintiff remains ready and willing to recover the amount with interest at the rate permitted by law and costs, until the refund is made. The subject-matter value for the suit is Rs. 20,00,000; interest on a compounding basis or as prescribed by law from the date of default until actual payment and costs will be claimed, and the court fee will be payable on the value of Rs. 20,00,000 in accordance with the Court Fees Act in force. Verification: I, the Plaintiff {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, do hereby verify that the contents of this Cause of Action are true and correct to the best of my knowledge and belief and that this plaint is being filed before the District Court within the jurisdiction, on {{FILING_DATE}}, at place_names, with the appropriate verification by the authorized person on behalf of the Plaintiff.


LIMITATION

The Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule provides Article 47 for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails, the period being three years and the accrual to occur on the date of the failure. In the present suit, the Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 as advance for a business transaction on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} and the entire transaction failed due to the Defendants default. The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} on account of the Defendants failure to perform or to refund the amount, the date of which is also reflected as {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. The suit was instituted in the District Court on {{FILING_DATE}}. Accordingly, the three-year limitation period from the accrual date ends after {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, and the present filing on {{FILING_DATE}} is within that period; thus the suit is within time.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

VALUATION AND COURT FEE
The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000, being the amount paid by the Plaintiff on {{ACCRUAL_DATE}} as an advance for a business transaction in {{DISTRICT}}, which failed due to the Defendants default, and the Plaintiff prays for refund with interest and costs. The suit is valued at Rs. 20,00,000 for the purpose of court fee and jurisdiction, being the claim amount. The Court Fee payable on the plaint under the applicable State Court Fees Act for a suit of money recovery is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}.


INTEREST

The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, entered into an agreement with the Defendant dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} and paid Rs. 2,000,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} as an advance for a business transaction. The Defendant failed to perform its obligations by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} and defaulted by not repaying the advance, resulting in the amount remaining with the Defendant as on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. The Plaintiff served a legal notice on {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding repayment; the Defendant did not repay, and the notice reply was received on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}. The cause of action accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and is continuing.

Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff claims pre-suit interest on the principal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 at 12% per annum for the period from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} on the ground that the amount was advanced for a commercial transaction and the Defendants wrongful retention constitutes breach of the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. The rate is justified as reasonable compensation for the use of funds in a commercial deal, reflecting the prevailing bank rate and the contractual understanding that the advance was to be repaid upon performance.

Pendente lite interest: The Plaintiff claims pendente lite interest, at 9% per annum, on the decretable amount from the date of filing the suit on {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree, under Section 34 CPC. Section 34 CPC empowers the court to award interest on money decrees during the pendency of the suit at a rate deemed appropriate by the court; the chosen rate ensures fair compensation for the use of funds during litigation and discourages undue delay.

Future interest: Post decree, the Plaintiff claims interest at 12% per annum from the date of decree until realization on the decretal amount. The rate is justified by the commercial context of the transaction and the objective of full compensation for the use and withholding of funds, with the Court empowered by Section 34 CPC to fix such interest from the date of decree to realization.

Section 34 CPC cited: Pendente lite interest is sought and justified with reference to Section 34 CPC, which authorizes the award of interest on a money decree during pendency and on the decretal amount from decree to realization.


PRAYER

The Plaintiff respectfully prays for the reliefs set out below against the Defendant in a suit for recovery of money and for total failure of consideration, it being alleged that the Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 (Rupees twenty lakh) as an advance for a business transaction which failed due to the Defendant�s default and the Defendant has not refunded the same despite demand. The Plaintiff further prays for interest and costs in terms of the provisions of law and for such other relief as this Honble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

(a) Decree for the principal amount of Rs. 20,00,000/-, being the advance paid by the Plaintiff to the Defendant under the contract for a business transaction concluded on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} and which completely failed due to the Defendant�s default, and directing the Defendant to pay the said principal sum to the Plaintiff.

(b) Pre-suit interest on Rs. 20,00,000 at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}} per annum from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} up to {{FILING_DATE}}, calculated in accordance with law, and to be paid along with the principal amount.

(c) Pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC on the amount of Rs. 20,00,000 at the rate of {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}} per annum from {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree or final disposal of the suit, as the debt remains due and payable during the pendency of these proceedings.

(d) Post-decree interest on the decretal amount at the rate of {{POST_DECREE_RATE}} per annum from the date of the decree until realization, in accordance with law, to ensure compensation for the Plaintiff	s loss and as a measure of finality and justice.

(e) Costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice to the Defendant, quantified at Rs. {{LEGAL_NOTICE_COST}}, with the total costs of the suit quantified at Rs. {{TOTAL_COSTS}} in all, and the Plaintiff prays that the Defendant be directed to pay the said costs in addition to the decretal amount.

(f) Any other relief which this Honle Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case, including such further and other reliefs as may be deemed just and equitable in the interest of justice, so as to secure complete redress to the Plaintiff.

General relief: The Plaintiff also prays for such further and other reliefs as this Honle Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case, including costs and interest on any amount decreed, as the Court may deem just and proper.

10. Serial No.: 3; Description: Legal notice sent to defendant; Annexure Label: Annexure C
11. Serial No.: 4; Description: Reply to legal notice (if any); Annexure Label: Annexure D

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.
